summary: Jeff Atwood argues that optimism, while seemingly positive, is actually an occupational hazard in software development that leads to project failures. Drawing on Kent Beck and Steve McConnell, he distinguishes between healthy optimism grounded in data and destructive wishful thinking that ignores reality. The post identifies two major optimism-related pitfalls: wishful thinking that undermines planning, and heroic can-do attitudes that prevent honest progress reporting. Atwood advocates for pragmatism over blind optimism, arguing that under-promising and over-delivering is the wiser approach. He concludes that acknowledging the unknown takes more courage than making unrealistic promises.
key_points: Optimism becomes dangerous when it transforms into wishful thinking—hoping something works without reasonable basis for believing it will
Developer heroics and can-do attitudes often prevent honest status reporting, turning minor setbacks into disasters
The real art of managing programmers lies in knowing when to demotivate them and rein in excessive enthusiasm
Wishful thinking at the beginning of a project leads to big blowups at the end and may cause more software problems than all other causes combined
Pragmatism and under-promising while over-delivering is braver and more effective than blind optimism
money_quotes: Optimism is an occupational hazard of programming.
The real art of managing programmers lies in knowing when to demotivate them.
Wishful thinking isn't just optimism. It's closing your eyes and hoping something works when you have no reasonable basis for thinking it will.
Wishful thinking undermines meaningful planning and may be at the root of more software problems than all other causes combined.
Can-do attitudes escalate minor setbacks into true disasters.
Management placed a higher premium on can-do attitudes than on steady and consistent progress and meaningful progress reporting.
It takes a lot more bravery to acknowledge the unknown than it does to blindly promise the world.
key_insight: In software development, the courage to acknowledge uncertainty and deliver honest assessments is far more valuable than the false bravery of optimistic promises.
